In the very earliest versions of my, our seed round in fact, said like, we think this technology is disruptively important. We think the most impactful areas for this to be deployed, of course, are in residence, just like the sort of global housing crisis, but even in the seed deck, we were saying, we think there's a profound opportunity here for the military and for military construction. And we think when it's time to build on other worlds, like, this is going to be like the only game in town when it comes to building in space. That sort of seemed crazy eight years ago, but in fact, that is what has been happening all along. And so we've been working with the military, the intelligence communities, and NASA for six or seven years now. And it has sort of finally come to a head where like that business inside of ICON will grow about 500% this year. And it's now worth like a dedicated focused effort because there's like interesting ways that you do business development with the government customer, there's interesting legal arrangements there. And of course, there's just like a sharp point on what the technology needs to do. Everybody wants faster and cheaper, but there's some things that are weighted more highly in a military and intelligence environment, you know, survivability...

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: What are they? Because I mean obviously building faster, cost for the military is not as important as for the single mom or dad we talked about earlier, but speed I would think—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Speed and quality.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: That's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Ballard
full_transcript.text: Yeah, speed is, speed is a big deal. And they have a special word for quality called survivability. I mean these have to be like quite safe depending on sort of the situations soldiers and and people in the military are in. And so they have this like extreme sensitivity to survivability and durability of the things that they build. They also are very sensitive to—they often operate in places where they don't have easy access to a complicated supply chain or a lot of skilled labor. And you know, a wall system, which is primarily the thing we automate with the technology today, you know, a typical wall system is going to take, you know, 15 to 20 people to build in about 60 days, and our system builds that same wall system in the residential space in about two people in a week.

And what they are missing, which they very sharply need, is a high-speed deployment of survivable structures with a simple supply chain without needing a bunch of skilled labor hanging around. And that is the tool that robotic construction offers. You have the automation of the labor through the robotics, and then you're building with a very simple supply chain in a material that is inherently survivable.

highlights: ICON is revolutionizing construction through large-scale 3D printing, enabling the production of high-performance military barracks and affordable homes for the homeless in a fraction of the traditional time and cost. The company’s "formcrete" technology provides superior survivability against modern threats like drones while allowing for architectural beauty that is typically reserved for high-end luxury properties.

Research indicates a critical link between housing, urban planning, and public health, with studies showing that commutes exceeding 30 minutes significantly increase stress, anxiety, and the likelihood of divorce. Prioritizing short commutes and dignified, aesthetically pleasing housing is foundational to addressing broader societal issues like substance abuse and domestic violence.

New decentralized AI models are achieving 98% accuracy in remote real estate appraisals, outperforming traditional in-person inspections and established digital platforms. These tools are being integrated into intelligence networks to provide investors and agents with high-fidelity pricing data without the logistical hurdles of physical site visits.

The next generation of AI wearables includes earbuds equipped with cameras that use binocular vision and on-device language models to interpret the wearer's surroundings. This technology offers transformative potential for assisting the visually impaired and providing travelers with instant, heads-up translations and environmental context.

Decentralized incentive structures are emerging as a disruptive alternative to proprietary software development, mobilizing global communities to build faster and cheaper tools. This model is currently being applied to diverse sectors, including content management systems and real-time AI agents, to challenge existing technology monopolies and reduce consumer costs.
summary:
This episode of *This Week in Startups* centers on the themes of disruptive construction, decentralized AI, and the "win-win-win" potential of technology for both investors and society. Guest Jason Ballard, founder of ICON, details the company’s expansion into military-grade 3D printing, explaining how their technology builds survivable, cost-effective structures like barracks in a fraction of the traditional time. The discussion also highlights ICON's mission to address the housing crisis by producing beautiful, $99k homes for the homeless, emphasizing that modern technology must move beyond "widgets" to solve foundational human needs like shelter and stability.

The transcript also explores the emerging BitTensor (Tao) ecosystem through guest Sebi Rubino of Rezi Labs, who discusses using decentralized AI subnets for highly accurate real estate appraisals. This technical deep dive is framed by a personal narrative from Jason Calacanis, who recounts being "shivved" for $500k by a founder who denied investors any equity during an acquisition. Rather than pursuing litigation, Calacanis announces a plan to "balance the ledger" by launching a decentralized, open-source Headless CMS on BitTensor. By leveraging a domain name purchased from the failed company, he aims to utilize the competitive nature of decentralized AI to offer a product that is significantly cheaper and more efficient than proprietary alternatives.

The episode concludes with a lighter "Off Duty" segment, focusing on hardware innovations and media critiques. The hosts discuss "Viewbuds"—earbuds equipped with AI cameras that provide binocular vision for the visually impaired or for real-time translation—and share recommendations for high-performance Anker charging gear. Finally, they review the new AMC show *The Audacity*, debating whether modern media portrayals of Silicon Valley are becoming overly derivative by focusing exclusively on cynical tropes of fraud and narcissism rather than the genuine efforts of builders to change the world.
